Title: Cockroach leg found in food: Penang Health Dept issues corrective orders
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: The Penang Health Department (JKNPP) has found the cleanliness level at a famous restaurant in Lorong Pitt to be satisfactory following an inspection, despite a recent viral complaint involving the premises.
Batu Uban assemblyman A. Kumaresan had raised concern over food hygiene enforcement after a
cockroach leg
was reportedly found in food served at the well-known eatery.
State Health Committee chairman Daniel Gooi Zi Sen said the investigation was carried out by the Food Safety and Quality Unit of the northeast district health office after receiving the complaint.
"The inspection on March 16 revealed that the premises maintained a satisfactory level of cleanliness and had undertaken improvement measures.
"However, several aspects still require further attention," he said today.
Gooi said corrective instructions had been issued to the restaurant operator to ensure full compliance with the Food Act 1983 and the Food Hygiene Regulations 2009.
He said the operator was also given health education on maintaining premises hygiene, safe food handling practices and adherence to existing legal requirements.
"The department remains committed to ensuring a high level of food safety in Penang.
"Monitoring and enforcement activities will continue periodically to safeguard public health," he added.
He also expressed appreciation to the public for their vigilance and cooperation in reporting complaints and providing information related to food safety.
The issue came to light after Kumaresan raised concerns over food hygiene enforcement, warning that neglecting cleanliness and food safety could lead to food poisoning.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Indians panic-buying fuel despite government assurances
Article:
AHMEDABAD: India's government says there are adequate fuel stocks despite shortages due to the Middle East war, but panicking citizens have triggered long queues at filling stations.
In retaliation for US and Israeli strikes that began more than three weeks ago, Iran has virtually closed the strategic Strait of Hormuz through which one-fifth of the world's oil passes, sending global energy prices soaring.
India, one of the world's largest crude oil importers, relies on foreign suppliers for more than 85 per cent of its oil needs – with Russia being the biggest supplier.
Retail prices in India have, however, held steady, with Prime Minister Narendra Modi's government saying it has "adequate crude inventories" and "sufficient stock of petrol and diesel."
"Don't believe rumours," India's top oil ministry official Sujata Sharma told reporters on Wednesday. "Don't indulge in panic buying."
But several parts of the vast country have witnessed just that nonetheless.
Police guarded fuel depots in the western state of Gujarat after sales reportedly doubled.
Private company employee Ashish Singh said he had to queue for nearly an hour
"I was given only Rs 2000 (US$21) worth of fuel," he told AFP. "They said no to full tanks."
He was not the only one forced to ration.
"I could only get Rs 300 (US$3) worth of petrol from one station, despite asking for more," said marketing executive Shailesh Prajapati, whose job involves long commutes on his motorbike.
Indian media report similar disruptions elsewhere.
Several pumps in the southern state of Telangana put up "no stock" signs after a surge in sales, the Hindustan Times newspaper reported.
Neighbouring Karnataka state also reported unusual queues, The Hindu reported, forcing several depots to shut.
Indian state-owned refiners issued statements Wednesday saying "rumours" about fuel shortages were "completely unfounded."
"There is no shortage of petrol or diesel," said Indian Oil Corporation Limited, the country's largest oil marketing company.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Rising diesel costs, subsidy delays squeeze bus operators
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Stagnant cash flow, rising maintenance, and subsidy gaps are crippling the local bus industry.
While the government continues to provide diesel subsidies for public transport through the Subsidised Diesel Control System (SKDS), industry leaders say the current mechanism and its scope are threatening the sustainability of many companies.
Pan Malaysian Bus Operators Association (PMBOA) president Datuk Ashfar Ali said even for those who receive the subsidy, such as express bus operators, the "pay-first, refund-later" model has created a severe liquidity crisis.
Under the SKDS, express buses are allocated a quota of 6,000 litres, extendable to 10,000 litres with the Land Public Transport Agency (Apad) support at a price of RM1.881 per litre.
However, Ashfar explained that the actual transaction at the pump is causing a bottleneck.
"Every company has a credit account with fuel suppliers. When we refuel, the amount is deducted at the market price.
"The government only refunds the price difference after the individual company's statement date," he told the New Straits Times.
"This means our cash flow is constantly 'stuck' until the end of the billing cycle. It is a significant burden for express bus operators who are already struggling," he added.
The situation is reportedly more dire for tourism, charter, and worker bus operators, who remain excluded from the subsidy programme.
Malaysian School Bus Operators Federation (GPBSM) president Mohd Rofik Mohd Yusof said the sudden jump in diesel prices to RM 5.52 per litre has left operators nationwide "extremely squeezed."
"Tourism and charter buses don't get the subsidy. We accepted bookings as early as December and January based on the diesel cost of RM2.15. When the price suddenly jumps, it creates a crisis with our customers," Rofik said.
He added that while a small number of customers are understanding, "99 per cent of them question why they have to pay more when a price was already agreed upon."
"We aren't trying to be rebellious. We just want the government to give some attention to tourism and charter buses during this period of sudden price hikes. We simply cannot operate with costs increasing so significantly," Rofik added, noting that operators in the Northern Zone and East Coast are already planning press conferences to voice their grievances.
Beyond fuel, the industry is also battling a "domino effect" of rising costs for imported spare parts.
Ashfar noted that the prices of lubricants, tyres, and batteries, many of which are petroleum-based or imported have already begun to climb.
This coincides with a 20 to 30 per cent drop in passenger volume following the conclusion of the festive season.
Both leaders called on the Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry to review the subsidy mechanism and scope to ensure the industry remains viable amidst the current global economic climate.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sunway wins near-unanimous vote for IJM deal, gets MACC clearance
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Sunway Bhd's proposed takeover of IJM Corp Bhd has received overwhelming shareholder backing, with 99.97 per cent voting in favour at its extraordinary general meeting today.
Founder and chairman Tan Sri Dr Jeffrey Cheah said the group has also received a letter from the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) clearing it of any investigation related to its acquisition of IJM shares.
More to come
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: RON95 ban on foreign vehicles to be enforced from April 1
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: Enforcement of a ban on the purchase of RON95 petrol by foreign-registered vehicles will take effect from April 1, Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Minister Datuk Armizan Mohd Ali said.
The move expands existing regulations, which previously placed liability only on petrol station operators, to now include drivers and owners of foreign-registered vehicles.
"Effective April 1, this offence will also include drivers or owners of foreign-registered vehicles," he said in a Facebook post.
Armizan said the enforcement follows a decision presented in Parliament on Jan 29 and will be carried out under OPS Tiris 4.0 (Integrated), aimed at curbing subsidised fuel smuggling, particularly in border areas.
"I am reminding all parties, whether petrol station operators or foreign vehicle users, to comply with this ban," he said, adding that authorities would not compromise with individuals, syndicates or operators involved in smuggling or subsidy abuse.
He also urged the public to act as the government's "eyes and ears" by reporting suspicious activities, in line with the Kita Gempur initiative.
Earlier, Armizan chaired a meeting with the enforcement division of the Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry to finalise preparations for the expanded enforcement.
In a related development, he said the government has assured that supplies of basic necessities remain sufficient despite rising logistics and packaging costs linked to the ongoing conflict in the Middle East.
The assurance follows engagement with the Malaysia Retail Association (MRA), aimed at strengthening cooperation between the government and industry to maintain supply stability and price control.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia should work with nations seeking diplomatic end to Iran war, says former minister
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Former foreign minister Datuk Seri Saifuddin Abdullah has called on Malaysia to actively engage with countries pursuing diplomatic solutions to the US-Israeli war against Iran.
Describing the conflict as an unjust war against a sovereign nation, he said Iran has the right to defend itself and respond.
"What is taking place is not just a 'conflict', but an unjust war against a sovereign nation," he said in a statement.
He said that efforts must go beyond a temporary ceasefire.
"What must be pursued is an end to the war that is just and ensures it will not recur, not merely a ceasefire," he added, saying any settlement must be based on terms agreed voluntarily by Iran, not imposed by the US and Israel.
He urged Malaysia to work closely with nations such as Pakistan, Turkiye and Qatar in pursuit of this goal.
"Malaysia should engage with countries that are committed to diplomatic efforts to end the war," he said, highlighting the importance of regional collaboration in seeking peace.
He also called for initiatives to restore peaceful coexistence between Iran and its neighbours, welcoming Malaysia's diplomatic outreach, including engagements with Iran and Oman.
"Malaysia is well-placed to contribute to peace efforts," he said.
Foreign minister Datuk Seri Mohamad Hasan said he had spoken to his Iranian counterpart Dr Seyed Abbas Araghchi on March 24 to discuss developments in Middle East, including the importance of safeguarding the Strait of Hormuz for global economic and energy stability.
He reiterated Malaysia's call for an immediate and comprehensive ceasefire, stressing that dialogue and diplomacy are key to easing tensions and ending the war.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Man sentenced to four months' jail for injuring pregnant wife
Article:
MELAKA: A man was sentenced to four months' jail for assaulting and injuring his pregnant wife.
Muhammad Aidil Azahar Rahmat pleaded guilty to two counts of voluntarily causing hurt before magistrate Nur Afiqah Radhiah Zainurin.
The court ordered the four-month prison sentences for each charge to run concurrently from the date of his arrest on March 19.
Aidil had previously been remanded over the alleged assault after his mother-in-law lodged a police report upon discovering the abuse through closed-circuit television (CCTV) footage.
She lodged the report after the victim became unreachable upon leaving the house with the accused and their 14-month-old son.
The court heard that Aidil assaulted his 20-year-old wife, Nur Azhiimah Abd Razak, inside and on the pavement of a karaoke outlet in Kota Syahbandar between 3.59am and 4.06am on March 15.
The charges were framed under Section 323 of the Penal Code, read together with Section 326A of the same Act, which carries a maximum penalty of two years' imprisonment, a RM2,000 fine, or both.
CCTV footage showed the accused punching, slapping and pulling his wife's hair. He was also seen striking her head with a motorcycle while she was lying on the ground.
Medical reports from Melaka Hospital confirmed the victim, who was pregnant at the time of the incident, suffered soft tissue injuries to her head and right cheek.
Police prosecuting officer Inspector Nur Zaidah Muhamad Zailani pressed for a deterrent sentence, arguing that the accused had a duty to protect his wife rather than inflict harm.
"As a husband, the accused should have protected the victim. The court should take into account the injuries sustained by the victim, who is carrying the accused's child," she said.
In mitigation, the unrepresented accused pleaded for a lenient sentence, telling the court he was remorseful for his actions.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MCOM Holdings offloads RM5mil liability-laden units for RM514
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: MCOM Holdings Bhd is divesting several loss-making subsidiaries with about RM5 million in net liabilities for a nominal RM514, as part of a strategic realignment.
In a bourse filing, the company detailed two conditional share sale agreements aimed at streamlining operations and sharpening its focus on core growth initiatives.
Under the first agreement, MCOM is disposing of its interests in MMT Group, comprising MCOM Messaging (Thailand) Co Ltd, Ivalent Co Ltd and Streamer Tech Co Ltd, to Chew Hwai Geeng for 1,000 Thai baht (about RM122).
MMT Group is primarily involved in providing mobile payment solution services in Thailand.
The second agreement involves the sale of MCOM's 100 per cent equity interest in MCatch (L) Bhd (MML), a Labuan-based mobile advertising platform provider, to Chew Sau Wai for US$100 (about RM392).
The decision to divest is driven by the subsidiaries' poor financial performance as at June 30, 2025.
On a standalone basis, key units such as MMT reported net liabilities of about RM6.94 million, with MML also in a capital deficiency position, bringing total standalone net liabilities to over RM10 million.
On an attributable basis, however, the group puts the combined audited net liabilities of the disposal at about RM5 million.
MCOM said the disposal will result in an estimated one-off pro forma loss of RM4.89 million, but will permanently remove the burden of recurring losses from these units.
Proceeds from the sale will be utilised as working capital for the group's remaining operations.
This move coincides with MCOM's efforts to strengthen its operational performance through new ventures, such as a recent digital advertising agreement between its subsidiary, MCOM Network Sdn Bhd, and AddVersion Inc.
"The proposals will allow the group to enhance its operational focus and reallocate its resources towards its core operations and growth initiatives.
"Although the disposal is expected to result in a one-off accounting loss, the group will no longer be burdened by recurring losses from the companies upon completion of the proposals.
"The proposals are expected to place the group in a better position to improve its financial performance and maximise shareholder value in the long run," it said.
The proposals are subject to shareholder approval at an upcoming extraordinary general meeting and are expected to be completed by the second quarter of 2026.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ferries sail on, bus operators feel the diesel squeeze
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: Ferry services in Penang will continue operating as usual despite the recent increase in diesel prices, authorities say.
Penang Port Commission chairman Datuk Yeoh Soon Hin said services between the Sultan Abdul Halim Terminal (PSAH) and Raja Tun Uda Terminal (PRTU) would not be affected.
"This is because the ferry service is a public service and a commitment from the government to the people," he told the New Straits Times, adding that operations would continue without changes.
The iconic car-carrying ferries were retired in 2020 and replaced with passenger-only vessels in August 2023. The ferry remains a key mode of transport in Penang, with a history spanning more than a century.
However, rising diesel prices are placing increasing pressure on other transport operators.
Several school bus operators said they are already feeling the impact, with some considering fare increases or suspending operations.
One operator, who identified herself as Khor, said she plans to raise fares by RM10 per student. She operates 20 buses but receives government subsidies for only eight.
"With diesel prices now exceeding RM5 per litre, it is becoming difficult to sustain operations. We are already operating on thin margins, and this increase will push us into losses," she said.
"If we increase the fares, parents will come after us. But what can we do?"
Khor added that the cost of a full tank has more than doubled, from just over RM70 previously to more than RM160 now, while maintenance expenses are also rising.
"Many operators are struggling. We need support," she said.
Similar concerns were raised by Northern Zone Tour Bus Operators Coalition chairman Ahmad Ruslan Abdul Latiff, who said many of his members have temporarily halted operations.
He said the coalition, which represents about 200 operators with 1,800 buses, has seen trip cancellations due to the higher cost of diesel, now at RM5.52 per litre.
"The price increase is too heavy for small operators. We are unable to cover operating costs and remain viable," he said.
Ruslan said the group has written to the tourism minister seeking assistance, warning that prolonged cost pressures could threaten the industry's ability to support Visit Malaysia Year.
He added that the tour bus sector plays a vital role in both domestic and international tourism.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Main-Market bound MTT Shipping upbeat on growth despite geopolitical headwinds
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: MTT Shipping and Logistics Bhd (MTTSL) remains confident in its outlook despite ongoing geopolitical pressures, underpinned by resilient cargo volumes and firm demand across its Asian service network.
Managing director Ooi Lean Hin said global trade flows outside the Gulf region have stayed largely stable, helping to cushion the broader impact of disruptions.
At an operational level, MTTSL's fleet continues to run at a high utilisation rate, with all 15 of its vessels deployed within its own service network being fully operational, while a further 11 ships are chartered out to major liner operators, reflecting steady demand for capacity.
Freight rates have also seen upward pressure, particularly across routes affected by rerouting and geopolitical risk premiums.
At the industry-wide level, tight vessel supply is also supporting charter rates, as the director noted that a limited pipeline of new ship deliveries relative to the number of ageing vessels exiting the market is expected to keep capacity constrained, particularly within the feeder segment.
This supply-demand imbalance, coupled with ongoing trade realignments, has strengthened the company's confidence in its expansion strategy.
Ooi said MTTSL is continuing with its fleet expansion plans following its planned Main Market debut on Bursa Malaysia this year.
"The company is well positioned to capture growth from shifting supply chains, as manufacturers increasingly relocate production to Southeast Asia amid geopolitical tensions and diversification strategies," he said.
However, Ooi cautioned that current disruptions are more localised compared with the extreme conditions seen during the Covid-19 pandemic, when global port congestion severely constrained capacity and drove freight rates up to tenfold.
"This situation is more localised. It still creates disruption, but not to the same extent as during Covid, where the entire global supply chain was affected," he said.
For now, shipping firms remain in a balancing act of managing rising costs and operational risks while positioning themselves to benefit from longer-term shifts in global trade.
MTTSL has set its initial public offering (IPO) price at RM1.03 per share, expecting to raise up to RM652.5 million.
Of the proceeds, RM624.7 million, equivalent to 95.7 per cent of the total amount, will be utilised for the acquisition of at least 12 container vessels within 3 years of the listing date, while the remaining 4.3 per cent, or RM27.8 million, will be allocated for the reimbursement of listing expenses within the first six months upon listing.
The IPO comprises 633.5 million issued shares, with 312.5 million shares allocated for Bumiputera investors approved by the Ministry of Investment, Trade and Industry (MITI), 258.5 million shares for Malaysian and foreign institutional investors, and 12.5 million shares reserved for the company directors, employees, and key contributors.
The remaining 50 million shares will be reserved for the Malaysian public via balloting, split evenly between Bumiputera and non-Bumiputera.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: More motorists switch to RON95 as RON97 sales fall up to 50pct
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Many motorists are switching to RON95, with RON97 sales falling by up to 50 per cent following the recent price hike.
Petrol operators said they are seeing a clear and immediate shift in consumer behaviour, with overall fuel consumption also declining.
Bumiputera Petrol Station Operators Association president Datuk Abd Aziz Sapian said industry feedback indicates RON97 sales have fallen significantly, with estimates ranging from about 20 per cent to over 50 per cent depending on location and customer profile.
He said demand for non-subsidised fuels such as RON97, diesel and other premium products is likely to soften further if prices approach or exceed RM5.
"We are seeing behavioural shifts, even traditionally less price-sensitive users are beginning to switch to RON95 where feasible," he told the New Straits Times today.
This follows the government's announcement that RON97 is now priced at RM5.15 per litre, up from RM4.55, while unsubsidised RON95 has increased to RM3.87 from RM3.27.
The subsidised RON95 price remains at
RM1.99 per litre
for the period from March 26 to April 1, while diesel in Peninsular Malaysia has risen to RM5.52 per litre from RM4.72.
Prices in Sabah, Sarawak and Labuan remain unchanged at RM2.15 per litre.
For diesel, Aziz said the trend is still developing, adding that recent volumes may not fully reflect actual demand due to the festive season.
He said some stations recorded unusually low daily volumes, making comparisons less reliable, with clearer patterns expected once normal activity resumes.
Aziz said weekly fuel price adjustments are also contributing to increasingly volatile demand.
Typically, sales spike ahead of anticipated price increases as consumers fill up, followed by a noticeable drop immediately after.
"This creates short-term demand distortion and makes daily sales patterns less predictable," he said.
Aziz said the trend is closely linked to the current subsidy structure, where only about 25 per cent of diesel users enjoy subsidised pump prices, another 25 per cent receive cash assistance but still pay market rates, while roughly 50 per cent are fully exposed to market pricing.
He said this means the majority of users are highly sensitive to price movements.
"Looking ahead, any policy adjustment such as reducing the subsidised quota could accelerate this trend.
"Consumers would reach subsidised limits faster and be exposed to market prices earlier, likely leading to further demand contraction," he said.
While higher fuel prices and subsidy restructuring may reduce the fiscal burden at the national level, Aziz said the impact at the retail level is already evident, with more price-sensitive and unpredictable consumer behaviour, a shift towards lower-cost fuel options, and increasing financial pressure on dealers.
He said this is exacerbated by a structural cost mismatch, where dealer margins remain fixed in sen per litre while transaction costs, such as the Merchant Discount Rate (MDR), are charged as a percentage of the transaction value.
He said that with digital payments now accounting for 80 to 90 per cent of transactions, this has created a mismatch where revenue remains fixed while costs rise automatically with fuel prices.
"As prices increase, MDR also rises in absolute terms, eroding dealer margins, with transaction costs in some cases taking up a significant portion of commissions.
"At the same time, dealers are facing compounded pressures, including lower and more volatile sales volumes, higher transaction costs, and increased working capital requirements," he said.
Aziz said with weekly price adjustments, any drop in fuel prices can lead to immediate losses on existing stock, while increases require more capital to maintain inventory levels.
Aziz described this situation as a "triple mismatch" for dealers - purchasing at market prices, selling partly at subsidised rates, and facing rising transaction costs.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: RM9.5mil paid under duress, not bribe, says Victor Chin
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Businessman Victor Chin Boon Long said a RM9.5 million payment made to the so-called "corporate mafia" group was not a bribe, but a "service fee" paid under pressure to stop ongoing harassment.
Chin said the payment was not made to settle any investigation, close any case or secure any favour from enforcement agencies. Instead, it was a "service fee" extracted under threat by the syndicate.
"I was told that the payment of RM9.5 million would be passed to the corporate mafia group to make them happy, stop the hostilities against my people, and give me a second chance to live," he said in a statement today.
Chin said the group had pressured him to surrender his shares for free and, to force compliance, had targeted those around him by freezing more than 500 bank accounts belonging to individuals and companies believed to be linked to him.
The freezing of accounts meant salaries could not be paid, while housing and car loans could not be serviced, plunging those affected into immediate financial and mental distress.
"I had no choice. I felt extremely guilty because these people were suffering simply because they knew me or knew of me.
"The greatest wealth in life is not money, but peace of mind. I would have done anything for the peace of mind of my people," he said.
Chin said he welcomed the establishment of a Royal Commission of Inquiry or any fair and independent investigation into the matter, but urged that it be conducted thoroughly and impartially, covering all parties involved and not limited to him alone.
He has been at the centre of a growing controversy involving allegations of a so-called "corporate mafia" and an ongoing police investigation.
Chin, who is
linked
to business interests associated with NexG Bhd — the sole supplier of Malaysia's passport booklets and identity cards — is being
investigated
under the Anti-Money Laundering, Anti-Terrorism Financing and Proceeds of Unlawful Activities Act (Amla) over alleged links to the group. He has denied any involvement.
Chin claimed he and his associates were instead subjected to sustained pressure by the group, alleging it had attempted to gain control of NexG Bhd through intimidation, including threats and repeated demands for the "delivery of shares".
He alleged that instructions related to these
demands
were conveyed through an intermediary identified as "Mr R", whom he claimed was a member of parliament linked to
PKR
and who had requested RM10 million in October last year in exchange for resolving the matter.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Yeoh orders probe into 'protection money' video in Wangsa Maju
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Federal Territories Minister Hannah Yeoh has directed that a police report be lodged over a viral video showing two men allegedly demanding "protection money" from a massage parlour in Wangsa Maju.
In a Facebook post, she said the footage had raised public concern and warranted immediate investigation.
"Our position is clear — there is zero tolerance for any form of protection money or middleman practices," she said.
Yeoh also urged the public to come forward with information or complaints to the Integrity Unit of the Federal Territories Department, Kuala Lumpur City Hall (DBKL), or the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) over any alleged misconduct involving officials under the ministry's purview.
In a separate statement, DBKL denied any connection to the individuals in the video, rejecting their claim that they were "under DBKL".

Title: Water levels fall below normal across Kelantan amid prolonged hot weather
Article:
KOTA BARU: Water levels across Kelantan have fallen below normal following prolonged hot weather, affecting dams and rivers statewide.
The Bukit Kwong dam in Rantau Panjang recorded a level of 15.47m, below its normal 16.76m.
Data from Public Info Banjir also showed several major rivers below normal levels, including Sungai Golok in Rantau Panjang at 3.23m compared with its normal 5m.
Sungai Golok in Kampung Jenob, Tanah Merah, recorded 18.23m, below the normal 19m, while Sungai Kelantan in Kuala Krai stood at 15.63m compared with its normal 17m.
Meanwhile, Sungai Galas in Dabong, Kuala Krai, recorded 25.28m against a normal 28m, while Sungai Kelantan at Tambatan Diraja in Kota Baru stood at 0.23m, below its normal one metre.
Sungai Melor in Bachok and Sungai Semerak in Pasir Puteh also recorded below-normal levels at 5.29m and 2m respectively.
The impact is already being felt on the ground, with about 2,000 residents in five
villages
near the Malaysia-Thailand border relying on Sungai Golok after wells ran dry.
Villagers said the problem began about a week before Hari Raya and worsened during the festive period, leaving them no choice but to source water from the river.
Despite the river's shallow level, they said they had to depend on it as piped water supply from Air Kelantan Sdn Bhd (AKSB) was also insufficient.
The affected areas include Kampung Tok Deh, Kampung Lubok Gong, Kampung Tersang, Kampung Banggol Kulim and Pekan Rantau Panjang.
Meanwhile, Bernama reported that residents in Kampung Tanah Putih have been experiencing water supply disruptions due to unstable pressure at the treatment plant and frequent main pipe bursts.
AKSB said the issue was linked to increased pressure following the Bukit Chupak water treatment plant's operations in February, particularly affecting areas supplied by the Sungai Ketil plant and causing near-daily bursts in Bandar Baru.
"Frequent main pipe bursts, along with a leak in the tank at SMS Sains Gua Musang, have also affected the stability of the main tank at the Sungai Ketil water treatment plant.
"Kampung Tanah Putih is a high-lying area at the end of the supply line, and when disruptions or frequent pipe bursts occur, it can take time for supply to return to normal," AKSB was quoted as saying.
To minimise disruptions, the company has been repairing burst main pipes, deploying water tankers, boosting output at the Sungai Ketil water treatment plant, and carrying out regular inspections and maintenance of valves.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Gruesome discovery of three family members dead in Sungai Petani
Article:
SUNGAI PETANI: Police discovered three family members dead in a house in Kampung Belau, Merbok, after a foul stench was reported.
Kuala Muda police chief Assistant Commissioner Hanyan Ramlan said police were alerted by the public at around 12.30am, reporting that the couple had not been seen for three days and a strong odour was coming from the house.
"Initial checks found the house in darkness, with a strong stench and the door locked from the inside.
"We forced entry and found the bodies of the husband and wife in the kitchen area, wrapped and covered with carpets and clothing," he said at a press conference at the Merbok police station today.
The victims were identified as Zikri Abdul Rahman, 65, his wife Suraya Man, 53, and her 29-year-old son, Muhammad Shakir Zufayri Abdul Aziz.
Hanyan said there were bloodstains in the living room and signs that attempts had been made to clean the area.
"A third body, believed to be the woman's son, was found upstairs lying in a pool of blood with injuries to the neck.
"The bodies of the couple are believed to have been there for more than 48 hours, while the 29-year-old man's body appeared more recent," he said.
Hanyan said initial investigations point to a murder case, with the suspect also believed to be dead.
"The incident is believed to have occurred between 8pm on Monday and the time the bodies were found early this morning," he said.
Several sharp weapons were found in a container near the third body upstairs.
He added the deceased son had a history of mental health issues between 2018 and 2019, but was not known to have caused disturbances to neighbours.
Another son of the woman, who serves in the armed forces, had returned to Johor on Monday after spending Hari Raya with the family.
Hanyan said five witnesses have been called in to assist investigations.
"All bodies have been sent to the Sultanah Bahiyah Hospital in Alor Setar for post-mortem. The case is being investigated under Section 302 of the Penal Code," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: EU experts want broad restrictions on 'forever chemicals'
Article:
HELSINKI: "Forever chemicals" pose a growing risk to health and the environment and should be broadly banned across the European Union, with some exceptions, EU experts said in findings published on Thursday.
The substances, officially called PFAS, a group of more than 10,000 chemicals used in non-stick pans, stain-proof carpets, and other products – and often end up tainting food, water and wildlife.
Two committees of EU experts on Thursday published findings that highlighted their effects and that called for broad restrictions on the per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS).
"PFAS pose growing risks to people and the environment. They are highly persistent, remaining in the environment for long periods, travelling long distances, contaminating groundwater and soil, while some cause serious health issues, such as cancer and reproductive harm," one of the committees said in a statement accompanying its findings.
The European Union is expected to put forward a proposal to ban PFAS, known as "forever chemicals", in everyday consumer products such as clothing and pizza boxes, with exemptions for strategic sectors, like the medical field.
A legislative proposal was initially promised for the end of 2025 but Brussels was waiting for opinions from the two committees set up by the European Chemicals Agency's (ECHA) – one on the risks associated with PFAS and one evaluating the economic and social impact of a ban.
The first committee, the Risk Assessment Committee (RAC), found "that PFAS pose growing risks to people and the environment."
To "address the risks" new regulatory measures at the EU level are necessary, it said.
For the scientific risk assessment experts of the RAC, a total ban on PFAS would be the most effective way to minimise their impact and that exemptions would cause additional "emissions, leading to an uncontrolled risk."
But, the Socio-Economic Analysis Committee (SEAC), which published a draft opinion that will go out for a 60-day review period and will be finalised before the end of 2026, said a blanket ban is "likely not proportionate," given the lack of alternatives to the chemicals in several fields.
The SEAC still supported a "broad restriction" but stressed the need for a "balanced approach" in light of the wide use of PFAS, and recommended "targeted" exemptions.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Firefighters battle blaze at recyclable materials site in Shah Alam
Article:
SHAH ALAM: A fire broke out at a pile of recyclable materials in an open area in Section 36 here, sending thick black smoke billowing into the sky.
Selangor Fire and Rescue Department assistant director of operations Ahmad Mukhlis Mukhtar said the department is currently battling the fire after receiving an emergency call at 1.24pm.
"The operations commander reported that the fire involved a pile of recyclable materials in an open area measuring approximately two acres (0.809ha).
"Firefighting operations are currently ongoing, with the fire station chief at the scene," he said in a statement.
He said a total of 31 personnel are involved in the operation, which also saw the deployment of fire engines and water tankers from Shah Alam, Klang Selatan, Andalas, Pelabuhan Klang, Klang Utara and Bukit Jelutong.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MIC stays with BN, cites stability, multiracial strength
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Malaysian Indian Congress (MIC) decision to remain with Barisan Nasional (BN) reflects a firm, forward-looking stance and the coalition's multiracial strength.
The decision was made at the party's Central Working Committee (CWC) meeting yesterday.
BN secretary-general Datuk Seri Dr Zambry Abd Kadir said the move is rooted in a long-standing history, from the Alliance era (1955–1973) to BN's formation in 1974.
He said the decision is backed by strong and rational justifications, including the need for stability and continuity amid an increasingly fragmented political landscape.
"People need a stable, experienced platform like BN, which has proven its ability to govern inclusively and pragmatically. BN is more than a coalition; it is a tested model of interethnic cooperation crucial for social harmony," he said in a statement today.
Despite challenges, BN is restructuring and renewing its approach to better respond to public needs.
"We reject divisive politics. Unity and solidarity cannot be sacrificed for short-term interests," he said.
Deputy president Datuk Seri M Saravanan said MIC will assess Indian community sentiment before making further decisions, following Perikatan Nasional (PN) coming under PAS leadership, with Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Samsuri Mokhtar as chairman.
Zambry said efforts to strengthen BN will continue, ensuring that any attempts to weaken the coalition could be addressed through internal strength and unity.
"Ultimately, this is about the future. Unity, moderation and responsibility remain key to building a stable and prosperous Malaysia," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ex-Taipei mayor jailed 17 years for corruption
Article:
TAIPEI: A former Taipei City mayor who once ran for the presidency was sentenced on Thursday to 17 years in prison for taking bribes and misusing political donations.
Ko Wen-je, the founder of the Taiwan People's Party (TPP), which has become a third force in Taiwanese politics, was found guilty by the Taipei District Court where dozens of supporters had gathered ahead of the verdict.
Ko, 66, was charged in December 2024 with corruption relating to a property development when he was Taipei's mayor.
He was also indicted for misusing donations for the TPP and a charity foundation, and breach of trust.
In addition to the combined 17-year sentence, Ko was banned from running for public office for six years, the court said.
Prosecutors had sought a combined jail term of 28 years and six months.
Ko, who served two terms as Taipei mayor from 2014 to 2022, denied wrongdoing, a member of his legal team told AFP on Thursday.
The property scandal concerned a redevelopment project that saw its floor area ratio substantially increased – done with the city government's approval which allegedly benefited the developer.
Ko received more than NT$17 million ($532,000) in bribes and was involved in the embezzlement of more than NT$68 million of political donations made to the TPP and a company linked to the party.
He also misused around NT$8.27 million in donations for a social welfare foundation for his presidential campaign.
Ko stood down as chairman of the TPP in Jan 2025, several months after he was detained, and was replaced by Huang Kuo-chang, who previously described the charges against the party's founder as "politically motivated."
Ko was released on NT$70 million bail in September.
Ahead of the verdict, supporter Kenny Yang said Ko was "falsely accused" and a victim of "political persecution."
"Regardless of the outcome, we will continue to support him and help him seek justice – that's certain," Yang, a 52-year-old IT worker, told AFP outside the court.
"We cannot allow Taiwan to become a society without a sense of right and wrong, without justice."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Girl, 12, wins RM6,000 over classroom fan accident
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A 12-year-old girl has been awarded RM6,000 after being injured when a ceiling fan fell on her in a classroom in 2022.
The student suffered a broken index finger and a chest injury but has since fully recovered.
The girl filed the claim in January 2024 through her father as her representative.
Selama magistrate Prabakaran Rajoo ordered the government and four others to pay the girl RM6,000 for pain and suffering, plus RM1,075 in costs.
Her name is being withheld to protect her identity, as she is under 18.
She named the school's headmaster, the Larut Matang and Selama district education officer, the Perak state education director, the education director-general, and the government as the first to fifth defendants.
She said the incident was caused by negligence, with the school and officials failing to maintain the ceiling fan and ensure pupil safety.
In his written judgment, Prabakaran said the court accepted her evidence and was satisfied her injuries were directly caused by the fallen fan.
"The first to fourth defendants failed to ensure the ceiling fan was properly maintained, breaching their duty of care to the pupil," he said.
The magistrate rejected the defendants' claims that the incident was sudden and unforeseeable, and that proper precautions and maintenance had been in place.
Prabakaran also rejected the pupil's claim for post-traumatic stress disorder, ruling she had not shown it existed on the balance of probabilities.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Builders call for govt support amid diesel price hike
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Master Builders Association Malaysia (MBAM) has called for urgent government support and collective action amid escalating cost pressures following the recent diesel price hike.
Its president Oliver HC Wee said the construction industry is grappling with a convergence of challenges driven by geopolitical tensions in the Middle East, which have disrupted global supply chains and driven up material and energy costs.
He said contractors across all levels — large, medium and small — are facing rising input costs, uncertain delivery timelines and tightening cash flow.
"Left unaddressed, these pressures risk affecting not only project delivery but also the broader economic ecosystem that relies heavily on the construction sector as a key driver of national development," he said in a statement today.
Diesel prices in peninsular Malaysia rose to RM5.52 per litre following a sharp increase in global oil prices, while prices in Sabah, Sarawak and Labuan remain unchanged at RM2.15 per litre.
Wee said the industry is seeking a series of targeted and timely measures from the government to ensure continuity and stability.
Among the proposals are temporary tax reductions or deferments related to construction services, targeted diesel subsidies, and financial assistance such as soft loans and guarantee schemes to ease cash flow constraints.
He also called for the introduction of mandatory price fluctuation mechanisms for key materials such as steel, cement and fuel, as well as assurances that new fiscal policies would not be applied retrospectively to existing contracts.
"We also need stronger payment mechanisms to ensure timely certification and disbursement," he said.
To stabilise supply chains, Wee asked the government to facilitate alternative sourcing through relaxed import requirements and expedited approvals, as well as to consider establishing strategic reserves for critical construction materials.
Wee added that project continuity must be safeguarded through recognition of extension of time (EOT) entitlements arising from supply disruptions, alongside temporary moderation of liquidated ascertained damages (LAD) and adjustments to ongoing government contracts where necessary.
"These measures are not merely industry support initiatives, they are essential steps to safeguard the timely delivery of national infrastructure and development projects," he said.
At the same time, he urged private sector clients and developers to adopt a more collaborative and pragmatic approach, including revisiting contract terms, practising fair risk-sharing and ensuring timely payments.
Wee said while the industry remains committed to delivering quality projects and supporting national growth, the current environment requires a recalibration of risk allocation and stronger coordination among all parties.
"This is not a challenge faced by contractors alone. It is a shared national concern that requires coordinated action from the government, industry players and private sector stakeholders alike.
"We are not seeking protection, but partnership to ensure that projects continue, businesses sustain, and the nation progresses," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Israel medics say Iranian missile attack leaves 6 wounded
Article:
TEL AVIV: The Israeli military said air defences responded to missile attacks from Iran on Thursday that according to medics left six people lightly wounded and caused some damage.
A spokesperson for Israel's Magen David Adom emergency medical service said paramedics were providing medical treatment to "six people who were lightly injured by blast effects", updating an earlier toll of two injured.
Earlier on Thursday, a spokesperson for MDA told public radio there was damage to several houses in the central city of Kafr Qassem, whose Mayor Haitham Taha said it was caused by cluster munitions.
The military said Home Front Command rescuers were called to an impact site "in central Israel."
In three separate statements in around 90 minutes, the military said it had "identified missiles launched from Iran toward the territory of the State of Israel."
"Defensive systems are operating to intercept the threat," the statements said, followed by announcements that people were "now permitted to leave protected spaces in all areas of the country."
Air raid sirens wailed across central Israel, including in Jerusalem and Israeli settlements in the occupied West Bank, while blasts were heard in Tel Aviv after the latest warning.
The attacks came more than 14 hours after the military last announced incoming missiles from Iran.
Central Israel came under fire overnight from Lebanon, where Iran-backed Hizbollah claimed a series of attacks targeting military sites. Israeli media said six rockets were intercepted.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Water supply in north stable for now, expert warns of rising risks
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Water resource systems serving northern states are holding, but with a narrowing margin for error, an expert has warned, as reservoirs in Kedah which support supply to Penang, Perlis and parts of Perak come under sustained pressure from prolonged dry weather.
Climate disaster and water resources management expert Dr Chong Khai Lin said while Malaysia is not facing
water bankruptcy
at the national level, the situation in the north points to rising regional risk.
Chong, who is director of the Disaster Management Institute at Universiti Utara Malaysia, said Kedah is currently experiencing clear water stress driven by extended hot and dry conditions, compounded by sustained demand from agriculture, domestic use and industry.
"Malaysia is not currently facing water bankruptcy at the national level, and in that sense, the Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Fadillah Yusof's statement is valid.
"However, this should not be interpreted as an absence of risk, particularly in the northern region," she told the New Straits Times.
Chong said the critically low levels at Muda Dam are a key concern, but stressed that the situation must be viewed within the broader Muda Agricultural Development Authority (Mada) system.
She said the presence of Pedu and Ahning dams continues to provide an operational buffer, allowing authorities to balance supply across multiple reservoirs.
"This is likely why authorities maintain that the situation remains under control. The system is not in a comfortable state.
"It is functioning, but under pressure, with a reduced margin for error if dry conditions persist," she added.
Checks on Mada's open-source data today showed that Muda Dam holds 10,050 acre-feet (8.04 per cent capacity), Pedu Dam 349,030 acre-feet (39.89 per cent), and Ahning Dam 121,089 acre-feet (54.30 per cent).
Combined, the three reservoirs stand at 480,169 acre-feet, or 39.26 per cent capacity.
Chong said the existing three-dam system appears sufficient to manage current conditions, provided operations are carefully coordinated.
However, she cautioned that relying solely on existing storage capacity may not be sustainable in the long term.
"Water security in northern Malaysia will increasingly depend on how well the system adapts to changing climate patterns, rising demand and rainfall variability," she said.
She said recent conditions in Kedah, including extended dry spells and temperatures reaching 38 to 39 degrees Celsius, point to a significant dry-hot episode rather than routine seasonal variation.
"While more data is needed to determine long-term trends, it is prudent to assume that such events may become more frequent or more severe, and planning should reflect that possibility," she said.
She said future efforts must focus on strengthening water security through more integrated and forward-looking measures.
These include establishing clear drought operating rules for reservoirs, optimising the three-dam system as a coordinated network using real-time data and forecasting, and improving basin-level coordination among agricultural, domestic and industrial users.
She said lessons from countries such as Japan show effective water management depends not only on storage capacity, but also on how systems are operated through predictive tools, flexible release strategies and structured coordination among stakeholders.
She also said efforts to reduce non-revenue water, protect catchment areas and promote water-use efficiency must be intensified.
"Equally important is risk communication, ensuring the public understands the seriousness of the situation and adopts conservation practices early," she added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: North Korea, Belarus sign 'friendship' treaty during Lukashenko visit
Article:
SEOUL: North Korea and Belarus's strongmen leaders signed a "friendship and cooperation" treaty on Thursday after Kim Jong Un gave a lavish welcome to President Alexander Lukashenko on his maiden visit.
Besides supporting Russia's war against Ukraine – around 2,000 North Korean soldiers are thought to have died – both nations are under Western sanctions and are accused of gross human rights violations. The two men met last year in China.
"In the modern realities of global transformation – at a time when the world's major powers openly ignore and violate the norms of international law – independent countries must cooperate more closely and consolidate their efforts aimed at protecting their sovereignty and improving the well-being of their citizens," Belarusian state news agency Belta quoted Lukashenko as saying.
"We oppose the illegitimate pressure on Belarus from the West and express our support and understanding for the measures taken by the leadership of Belarus aimed at ensuring social and political stability and economic development," Belta quoted Kim as saying.
Earlier Belta showed Kim and Lukashenko hugging at a lavish welcome programme on Wednesday at the start of the two-day visit involving an artillery salute and goose-stepping soldiers before a large flag-waving crowd.
Lukashenko visited the Kumsusan Palace of the Sun – where Kim's embalmed father and grandfather lie in state – to pay his respects, flanked by top North Korean officials, the Korean Central News Agency reported.
Lukashenko, 71, who has ruled Belarus since 1994 and has swung firmly behind Moscow since the start of the Ukraine war in 2022, also laid a bouquet on behalf of Russian President Vladimir Putin.
Belarus and North Korea are part of a push driven by Chinese President Xi Jinping and Putin to create what they call a "multipolar world" to challenge Western hegemony.
They have both provided Moscow assistance in its Ukraine war, with Minsk serving as a launchpad for the invasion and Moscow stationing tactical nuclear weapons in Belarus.
South Korean and Western intelligence agencies have estimated that the North has sent thousands of soldiers to Russia, primarily to the Kursk region, along with artillery shells, missiles and rocket systems.
Russia and North Korea signed a strategic partnership agreement in 2024 that obliges either side to provide "military and other assistance" should the other be attacked.
Analysts say North Korea is receiving financial aid, military technology, food and energy supplies from Russia, helping Pyongyang reduce its reliance on its long-time backer China.
US President Donald Trump has sought to build ties with Belarus in his second term, easing sanctions and welcoming it to his "Board of Peace."
Trump met Kim three times in his first term, and there has been speculation of a re-run when the US president makes his visit – delayed by the Iran war – to China on May 14-15.
Belarusian Foreign Minister Maxim Ryzhenkov said that in addition to the treaty of friendship and cooperation, the two sides would agree to cooperate in an array of fields from agriculture to information.
"Our greatest interest... is strengthening truly friendly, partnership relations. We have friends here, and they are waiting for us. Just as we await them in Belarus," he told state news agency BelTA.
Trade between the two countries is "modest", but areas for growth include Belarus exporting pharmaceutical products and food to North Korea, Ryzhenkov said.
"Meanwhile, various cosmetic products, which are renowned for their quality and affordable prices, can be imported from the DPRK," he added, using the initials of the North's official name.
The visit is intended to "show solidarity" among nations opposed to the Western order, Lee Ho-ryung of the Korea Institute for Defence Analyses told AFP.
"Kim will try to use the occasion to raise its diplomatic profile and strengthen solidarity among the so-called anti-Western bloc," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: King tells public to take extreme caution amid heatwave
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: His Majesty Sultan Ibrahim, King of Malaysia, has advised the public to exercise extreme caution and take necessary health precautions as the nation continues to grapple with a prolonged heatwave.
In a posting via his official Facebook page today, His Majesty emphasised the importance of staying hydrated and minimising outdoor exposure to mitigate health risks associated with high temperatures.
Expressing concern for the younger generation, Sultan Ibrahim called on the Education Ministry to take proactive measures to safeguard the well-being of students.
With weather experts predicting the heatwave to persist until early or mid-June, His Majesty stressed that schools must remain vigilant to ensure students are not adversely affected by the extreme conditions.
"The elderly and children, particularly those with chronic illnesses, are advised to avoid direct heat exposure to prevent any medical complications," His Majesty said.
The King also highlighted the increased risk of fire outbreaks during this period.
He urged the Fire and Rescue Department to heighten its level of preparedness to manage potential emergencies.
"At the same time, the public is strongly advised to avoid any open burning, as the current hot and windy conditions can cause fires to spread rapidly," he added.
Sultan Ibrahim extended his appreciation to the nation's security and rescue agencies.
He lauded the Fire and Rescue Department, the Civil Defence Force (CDF), and healthcare personnel for their tireless commitment and readiness in facing any eventuality throughout this challenging weather period.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Caretakers in fireworks stall fire case released on police bail, probe ongoing [WATCH]
Article:
NIBONG TEBAL: Three caretakers, including a woman, detained to assist in investigations into a fireworks stall fire in Taman Pekaka have been released on police bail.
Penang police chief Datuk Azizee Ismail said the trio were released as investigations are still ongoing, with several more witness statements to be recorded before the case is referred to the Deputy Public Prosecutor (DPP).
"They have been released on police bail.
"We still need to record statements from two to three more witnesses and are awaiting the Fire and Rescue Department's report before referring the investigation paper to the DPP," he told the New Straits Times today.
The trio were initially remanded for three days from March 23.
They were detained following a fire that destroyed a
fireworks stall
in Taman Pekaka.
The case is being investigated under Section 286 of the Penal Code for negligent conduct involving explosive substances.
Police have received a total of five reports related to the incident.
The Sunday midnight blaze destroyed the stall and damaged two cars, a motorcycle, an adjacent restaurant and a house.
Plumes of smoke from the fire could be seen from a distance.
No casualties were reported.
Penang Fire and Rescue Department director Mohamad Shoki Hamzah told the NST that
investigations
are expected to be completed within 14 days, although findings may be released earlier if ready.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hormuz gambit - How Teheran rewrote the rules of geopolitical power
Article:
FOR decades, Western strategists maintained a quiet consensus: Iran could threaten to close the Strait of Hormuz, but it could never truly do so.
The costs, they argued, would be catastrophic for Tehran itself.
The United States Navy stood ready. The "rules-based international order" would hold. That consensus, it now appears, was less a strategic assessment than a carefully managed story — and the story has begun to unravel.
Over the past month, Iran has not merely demonstrated its capacity to disrupt traffic through the strait.
It has gone considerably further, transforming one of the world's most vital maritime corridors into what analysts are calling a sovereign revenue mechanism — reportedly collecting transit fees of up to two million US dollars per oil tanker.
Roughly one-fifth of the world's petroleum supply moves through these waters. The arithmetic of leverage, suddenly, looks very different.
A battle fought in two arenas
To understand what has happened in the Strait of Hormuz, it is necessary to recognise that two separate contests were unfolding simultaneously: one on the water, and one in the information space.
In the weeks preceding the effective closure of the strait, Western media employed what communications scholars term reverse agenda-setting — directing public attention not toward the strategic significance of Hormuz, but toward American naval capabilities and coalition-building efforts.
The implicit message was reassuring: the problem, if there was one, was already being managed. The chokepoint itself was quietly downgraded in public consciousness.
Simultaneously, political figures including President Donald Trump sought to pre-empt Iranian action through what strategists describe as narrative anticipation — dismissing imagery of damage to American vessels as artificial intelligence fabrications and framing Iranian manoeuvres as theatrical bluster.
In psychological operations doctrine, this technique aims to neutralise an adversary's deterrent credibility before any physical action is taken.
The language deployed by Western officials reinforced this architecture.
Phrases such as "international freedom of navigation coalition" served to reframe a profound vulnerability — the world's near-total dependence on a single maritime corridor — as a manageable security coordination challenge.
In media studies, this is known as reality inversion: renaming a phenomenon to reshape public understanding of its gravity.
What has emerged from the fog of competing narratives is a significant shift in Iran's strategic posture.
Classical deterrence theory, associated with scholars such as Herman Kahn and Thomas Schelling, distinguishes between denial deterrence — the threat of retaliation against attack — and something more active: the capacity to impose costs on adversaries before any strike is launched, and to profit from the imposition.
Iran has moved into this second category. By collecting transit fees and establishing de facto administrative control over passage rights, Teheran has transformed the strait from a potential battlefield into an ongoing revenue stream.
This represents a sophisticated convergence of what political scientists call smart power — the combination of hard coercive capacity, economic leverage, and narrative control.
Critically, this is precisely the triad that Western powers, particularly the United States, have long deployed through the mechanism of international sanctions. The novelty lies in Teheran having adapted it to geography rather than finance.
[[nid:1404042]]
The psychology of denial
The persistent refusal of the Trump administration to publicly acknowledge the altered reality in Hormuz is not, analysts suggest, a product of ignorance. It reflects instead a well-documented phenomenon in political psychology: face-saving strategy under conditions of cognitive dissonance.
The political brand Trump has constructed over decades rests on decisive dealmaking, American strength, and the assurance that no adversary humiliates the United States without consequence.
Acknowledging that Iran has closed the strait, that Washington has failed to assemble an effective counter-coalition, and that Teheran is now financially benefiting from the arrangement, would require a fundamental revision of that brand narrative.
The political cost of such an admission, in the calculus of his advisers, exceeds the cost of denial — at least in the short term.
In the language of game theory, Trump has invested so heavily in a particular strategic story that revising it would be perceived as equivalent to forfeiting the game entirely.
It is, as one formulation has it, the behaviour of a chess player who, having lost his queen, insists he never had one.
The deeper question
Beyond the immediate crisis, the events in Hormuz raise a more uncomfortable question for Western policymakers: what happens when the rules-based international order — an order premised on American stewardship of critical global waterways — encounters a state that has rewritten the rules of engagement from within?
Iran has demonstrated that power in the contemporary geopolitical environment is not simply a function of military capability.
It is the capacity to make reality expensive for others — to alter the economic and strategic calculus of a region in ways that serve one's own interests. Teheran has done precisely that.
Whether this achievement proves durable is a separate matter. Military theorists would note that deterrence gains achieved through conventional means remain vulnerable to reversal as technological balances shift.
What is not in doubt is that the Strait of Hormuz has become something new: a laboratory for a theory of power that the West believed was its own exclusive instrument.
The tollbooth is open. The question now is how long others will keep paying — and whether Washington will ever admit it exists.
* The writer is senior lecturer, Faculty of Communication and Media Studies, Universiti Teknologi MARA (UiTM)
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Firefighters respond to mercury spill at Penang dialysis centre
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: A broken blood pressure machine caused a mercury spill at the Buddhist Tzu Chi Dialysis Centre in Penang, prompting a fire department response.
Penang Fire and Rescue Department assistant operations director John Sagun Francis said the incident involved a blood pressure machine that had broken, causing a mercury spill.
"Initial findings indicated the unit was in the process of being disposed of.
"Firefighters wore protective gear before cleaning up the spill using sulphur powder," he said in a statement.
The incident happened on the third floor of the building, which was empty at the time.
The fire department received an emergency call at 8.50am and dispatched personnel from the Lebuh Pantai and Bayan Baru fire stations.
John said the spilled mercury was collected and placed in chemical waste bags, along with equipment that could not be safely reused.
"The team then cleaned the affected area using a bleach solution," he added.
John said no one was hurt in the incident and the building has been declared safe after checks.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Anwar: Malaysia, China to strengthen diplomatic and security relations
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia and China have pledged to further strengthen diplomatic relations and security cooperation to safeguard the social and economic prosperity enjoyed by both nations amid current and future global challenges.
Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim said the matter was discussed during a courtesy call by China's Minister of State Security Chen Yixin, who is on his first visit to China's good-friend country, Malaysia, this year.
"Our discussions focused on bilateral relations, particularly security cooperation between our two countries, as well as the current global geopolitical landscape, which calls for close collaboration to ensure the continued stability and security of the region," Anwar posted on Facebook.
The prime minister also expressed hope that the progress achieved jointly across various sectors would continue to drive closer ties between Malaysia and China.
He added that, as a close partner, the presence of a senior Chinese government leader is highly valued by both himself and the entire Madani government.
– BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: SCA Solutions plans ACE Market listing to fund Kapar facility
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: SCA Solutions Bhd has filed for an initial public offering (IPO) on the ACE Market of Bursa Malaysia, with proceeds to be used for expansion and operational enhancement.
The group, which provides heating, ventilation and air conditioning (HVAC) and fire safety instrumentation solutions, plans to issue 114 million new ordinary shares.
Of these, 28.5 million shares will be offered to the Malaysian public, while 5.7 million shares are allocated to eligible directors and employees.
Another 71.2 million shares will be placed out to Bumiputera investors approved by the Investment, Trade and Industry Ministry, with 8.6 million shares allocated to selected investors via private placement.
The IPO also includes an offer for sale of 56.9 million existing shares to selected investors by way of private placement.
Malacca Securities is the principal adviser, sponsor, underwriter and placement agent.
SCA Solutions specialises in HVAC, fire safety and water treatment systems for buildings.
The company is led by managing director Tan Cheng Eng and executive director Liong Chian Leek, both UK-trained mechanical engineers.
A key focus of the IPO proceeds is the development of an integrated facility in Kapar, Selangor, to support the group's next phase of growth.
The facility will house a new corporate office for its subsidiary Greneco, along with a logistics and warehouse hub, and offices for sales and marketing.
It will also include a customer experience centre and demonstration laboratory to showcase its HVAC and fire safety solutions.
Currently, SCA Solutions operates from four premises in Selangor, including its headquarters in Kayu Ara, two additional offices in the same area, and a workshop in Shah Alam.
The new Kapar facility is expected to consolidate and expand its operations, while improving efficiency and strengthening its market presence.
Part of the proceeds will also be used to repay a term financing facility linked to the Kapar property, fund renovation works, support working capital needs, and cover listing expenses.
The group also plans to increase inventory levels to support the expansion of its HVAC and fire safety business, improving product availability and fulfilment timelines.
In addition, SCA Solutions intends to step up its sales and marketing efforts across Malaysia over the next 36 months, including direct engagement with customers and consultants, leveraging referrals, and enhancing its digital presence.
The group expects these initiatives to strengthen brand visibility and support long-term growth following its listing.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia, Singapore reaffirm close coordination amid rising West Asia tensions, says Lawrence Wong
Article:
SINGAPORE, March 26 — Singapore and Malaysia have reaffirmed their commitment to close coordination amid escalating tensions in West Asia.
In a Facebook post on Thursday, Singapore Prime Minister Lawrence Wong said he and his Malaysian counterpart Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim had a constructive exchange following a phone call on the latest developments in West Asia on Wednesday.
He said the discussion covered the risks of escalation, its impact on global oil supplies, and the need to safeguard livelihoods and regional stability.
He added that cooperation would be pursued both bilaterally and through the Asean regional bloc.
“Singapore and Malaysia will continue to coordinate closely on issues that matter to both our countries,” he said.
Wong is currently on a working visit to the People’s Republic of China from March 25 to 28 to attend the 2026 Bo’ao Forum for Asia (BFA) Annual Conference in Hainan. — Bernama

Title: Four‑month jail term for delivery rider who punched, slapped pregnant wife in Melaka
Article:
MELAKA, March 26 — A food delivery rider was sentenced to four months in prison by the Ayer Keroh Magistrate’s Court here today after pleading guilty to two charges of causing injury to his pregnant wife.
Magistrate Nur Afiqah Radhiah Zainurin handed down the sentence of four months’ jail on each count on Muhammad Aidil Azahar Rahmat, 22, and ordered the jail term to be served concurrently from the date of arrest on March 19.
The man was charged with voluntarily causing hurt to his wife, Nur Azhiimah Abd Razak, 20, by punching the back of her head.
He was also charged with slapping the wife on the face, striking her head and pushing her until she fell.
The offence was committed at a karaoke centre in the Kota Syahbandar Business Complex in the Melaka Tengah district on March 15, at 3.59 am and 4.06 am respectively.
He was charged under Section 323 of the Penal Code, read together with Section 326A, which provides a jail term of up to two years or a fine of up to RM2,000, or both, upon conviction.
The prosecution, led by Inspector Nur Zaidah Muhamad Zailani, urged the court to impose a sentence that would serve as a deterrent, taking into account the victim’s condition as she was pregnant at the time.
Muhammad Aidil Azahar, unrepresented, appealed for leniency, citing his mother’s poor health as she is suffering from a stroke.
Based on the facts of the case, the victim’s mother viewed the CCTV footage at the premises after being informed of the incident. The footage showed the accused assaulting the victim, including hitting, slapping and pulling her hair.
She later found her daughter at Melaka Hospital’s red zone before bringing her home. However, the victim was later discovered missing, with signs of forced entry at the back door of the house.
Fearing for her daughter’s safety, along with her one-year-old and two-month-old grandson, who was with the accused, the victim’s mother lodged a police report. — Bernama

Title: Court awards RM6,000 to Year Two pupil injured by falling classroom fan in Selama school
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 26 — The Selama Magistrate’s Court in Perak has ordered the government and four other parties to pay RM6,000 in damages to a Year Two pupil who was injured after a ceiling fan fell on her during a classroom lesson.
Magistrate R. Prabakaran made the ruling after allowing a lawsuit filed in January 2024 by the girl’s father against the school principal, the Larut Matang and Selama District Education Office, the Perak State Education Department, the Director-General of Education, and the Malaysian Government.
The court found that the first to fourth defendants had failed to take reasonable steps to ensure the ceiling fan was safe and properly maintained.
The damages awarded cover general damages for pain and suffering sustained by the plaintiff, who is now 12 years old. However, claims for special, punitive and exemplary damages were dismissed.
In the written judgment dated March 23, 2026, Prabakaran held that the risk of a ceiling fan falling in a classroom was reasonably foreseeable.
“The failure did not occur without cause but is usually linked to factors such as poor maintenance, wear and tear, or faulty installation. Therefore, it is a risk that could reasonably be anticipated by the school,” he said.
The court also rejected the school’s reliance on informal “see and listen” checks by teachers to detect faults, noting that such measures were insufficient.
Although the defence claimed regular maintenance had been carried out, no maintenance records or reports from the Public Works Department (JKR) were submitted in court to support those claims.
The magistrate stressed that such documents were crucial in determining the actual cause of the incident.
The court accepted medical testimony confirming that the pupil suffered a fracture to the second metacarpal bone of her left hand, as well as soft tissue injuries to the chest, directly caused by the incident. It was also noted that she has since fully recovered.
The incident occurred at about 10 am on Oct 18, 2022, when a ceiling fan fell in a classroom at a primary school in Selama, injuring the pupil and causing minor injuries to three other students. — Bernama

Title: RON95 ban on foreign vehicles kicks in April 1 as govt tightens fuel‑subsidy controls, says Armizan
Article:
PUTRAJAYA, March 26 — Enforcement of the ban on the purchase and sale of RON95 petrol to foreign-registered vehicles will take effect on April 1, said Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Minister Datuk Armizan Mohd Ali.
He said the move was a strengthening of the existing legal framework which previously only imposed offences on petrol station operators.
“Effective April 1, 2026, this offence will also include drivers or owners of foreign-registered vehicles,” he said in a post on his Facebook page here today.
Armizan said the enforcement was a follow-up to the decision he presented in Parliament on Jan 29.
He said the enforcement agenda would continue to be strengthened through the implementation of OPS Tiris 4.0 (Integrated) to ensure compliance with regulations and combat smuggling of subsidised fuel, especially in border areas following the tension in the Middle East conflict.
“I am reminding all parties, whether petrol station operators or foreign motor vehicle consumers, to comply with this ban,” he said.
Armizan said KPDN would not compromise with any individual, syndicate or operator involved in smuggling or abuse of fuel subsidies.
At the same time, Armizan called on the public to continue to be the eyes and ears of the government by channeling information regarding suspicious activities, in line with the KITA GEMPUR agenda to ensure that the interests of the people continue to be protected and that subsidy leakage can be curbed.
Earlier, Armizan chaired a meeting with the Enforcement Division of the Ministry of Domestic Trade and Cost of Living (KPDN) to finalise preparations for the enforcement of the extension of the ban on the purchase and sale of RON95 petrol to foreign-registered vehicles.
In a related development, Armizan said the government also guaranteed that the country’s supply of basic necessities would remain sufficient despite the increased costs of logistics and packaging materials due to the conflict in the Middle East.
He said the guarantee was achieved as a result of strengthening cooperation between the government and the retail industry through an engagement session with the Malaysia Retail Association (MRA), which is the main umbrella organisation for players in the sector.
“The main agenda discussed was the issues arising from the Middle East conflict, especially the implications for security of supply and price stability.
“Through strengthening cooperation between the government and the Industry, the supply of imported basic necessities is sufficient despite facing several issues, especially increasing logistics costs and packaging materials,” he said. — Bernama

Title: Police seek public help to locate 13-year-old girl reported missing in Bandar Baru Ampang
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 26 — Police are seeking public assistance to trace a 13-year-old girl, Nur Sofea Haton Abdul Hafiz, who was reported missing from her home in an apartment in Bandar Baru Ampang, Selangor, yesterday.
Ampang Jaya police chief ACP Khairul Anuar Khalid said the girl was discovered missing at about 7 pm when her mother returned home, with checks finding she had left the house with a suitcase, believed to be due to a misunderstanding with her family.
“A police report was lodged by her family at 12.15 am today,” he said in a statement here today.
Khairul Anuar said Nur Sofea is about 160 centimetres tall, weighs 56 kilogrammes, is of slim build and has a medium-dark complexion.
Those with information are urged to contact Insp Nur Alia Dyana Raimi at 017-6515201 or the Ampang Jaya district police headquarters (IPD) control centre at 03-42897222, or visit the nearest police station. — Bernama

Title: Viral video of alleged solicitation at Wangsa Maju massage centre sparks police probe
Article:
PETALING JAYA, March 26 — Authorities have launched an investigation after a video showing two men allegedly soliciting bribes from a massage centre in Wangsa Maju, Kuala Lumpur, went viral.
The clip, which runs for over eight minutes, reportedly shows the men claiming to be members of the Federal Territory Residents Representative Council (MPPWP) asking the business owner for money in exchange for allowing the operation to continue.
A timestamp on the footage, believed to have been captured via CCTV, indicates it was recorded on Tuesday (March 24).
Minister in the Prime Minister’s Department (Federal Territories) Hannah Yeoh, in a statement on her Facebook page said a police report had been filed.
She said she had also instructed and that Kuala Lumpur City Hall (DBKL) to investigate the matter immediately.
“If the allegations are proven true, the MPPWP members involved will be dismissed without compromise. Our position is clear: there is zero tolerance for protection money schemes or the use of middlemen,” she said.
